A dress worn for weddings and other events by Muslim and Jewish women in the Turkish region. The name Bindalli means thousands of branches, which are depicted in the embroidery. Dresses like this one were mostly owned and worn by wealthy women due to the extensive embroidery in gold fabric, but this type of dress was common among many economic classes of women.

This piece was originally made as part of an ophrey (or an altar frontal). Has a Netherlandish technique in this work, using gold to provide glitter and a 3-dimensional quality. The image is depicting the Virgin Mary is kneeling before her prayer book with the archangel Gabriel at her side, telling her of the coming birth of Jesus.

column8: Boston, Massachusetts, United States of America
column9: Agnette Jackson, daughter of prominent Black Abolitionists. She attended school at The Garrison Juvenile Society, where she learned to read, write, and do basic sewing. She recited,  “On religion”, which is embroidered onto this piece when the school recently gained funds for needlework.
